required_changes: Clarify the rationale behind choosing fixed per-series held-out sets and how this affects the generalizability of the results.
Specify the exact number of training samples (2000) in the baseline registry to ensure transparency and allow for a fair comparison with other models.
Include a detailed description of the data preprocessing steps, especially regarding the one-hot encoding of DNA sequences, to ensure reproducibility.
Provide more information on the architecture details of both CNN and MLP models, such as number of layers, neurons per layer, activation functions, etc., to facilitate comparison and understanding.
Explain how the random sampling policy is implemented and why it was chosen over other possible sampling methods.
Clarify the definition and implementation of 'fixed_splits'. Ensure that the splits are truly independent across different runs to avoid data leakage.
Provide a detailed explanation of how the random sampling policy is implemented, including any potential sources of variability or bias.
Include additional baseline models for comparison, such as decision trees or support vector machines, to provide a broader context for the performance of CNNs and MLPs.
Specify the exact version of the libraries used (e.g., TensorFlow, scikit-learn) and the hardware specifications to ensure reproducibility.
Document any preprocessing steps applied to the DNA sequences before one-hot encoding, as these could influence model performance.
Clarify the biological relevance of using a fixed per-series held-out set for validation, especially in the context of protein expression prediction.
Consider adding a baseline model that incorporates translation or other biological steps to better reflect the actual process of protein synthesis from DNA sequences.
Explain how the choice of one-hot encoding affects the ability of CNNs to capture spatial dependencies in DNA sequences compared to MLPs.
Clarify the rationale for choosing 'fixed_splits' as a required control. Explain why this is necessary and how it affects the comparison between RF and MLP.
Specify the exact number of trees in the Random Forest model to ensure reproducibility and avoid potential overfitting issues.
Provide more details on the architecture of the Multi-Layer Perceptron, such as the number of layers and neurons per layer, to ensure a fair comparison with the RF.
Include a discussion on how the choice of 'one_hot' feature set might impact the performance of both models and whether alternative encoding schemes should be considered.
Clarify the definition of 'fixed per-series held-out sets' to ensure consistency with Nikolados et al. (2022).
Provide a detailed explanation of how the random sampling policy is implemented, including any specific parameters or strategies used.
Include a description of the data preprocessing steps, such as normalization or scaling, applied to the one-hot encoded feature set.
Specify the exact number of trees and depth for the Random Forest model to ensure reproducibility.
Explain how the Multi-Layer Perceptron architecture is defined, including the number of layers, neurons per layer, activation functions, and any regularization techniques used.
Clarify the biological relevance of using a fixed split for protein expression prediction. Consider whether this approach aligns with standard experimental design practices in molecular biology.
Justify the choice of one-hot encoding for feature set. Discuss potential limitations and alternatives, such as k-mer frequency or sequence-based embeddings.
Explain the rationale behind random sampling policy. Consider if other sampling strategies could provide more robust results.
Clarify the rationale for using a fixed per-series held-out set and ensure that this method is appropriate for the dataset size and nature.
Specify the exact number of epochs, batch sizes, and other hyperparameters used for training both CNN and RF models to ensure reproducibility.
Include a detailed description of how the Random Forest model is configured (e.g., number of trees, maximum depth) and any preprocessing steps applied to the data before training.
Consider adding a baseline comparison with a more complex CNN architecture or other machine learning models to better understand the relative performance of different architectures.
Clarify the random seed policy for reproducibility.
Specify the exact version of libraries and frameworks used to ensure reproducibility.
Include a detailed description of how the fixed splits are generated and held out.
Provide more information on the preprocessing steps applied to the DNA sequences before one-hot encoding.
Clarify the biological relevance of using a CNN for protein expression prediction from DNA sequences, especially given that CNNs are traditionally used for spatial data.
Justify why one-hot encoding is chosen over other feature representations (e.g., k-mer counts) and how it aligns with the biological understanding of DNA sequence information.
Explain the rationale behind using fixed splits for evaluation and discuss potential biases introduced by this approach, such as temporal or batch effects in the data.
